coredata.dc:title: Gold nanocage-carbon nanodot composites activate ornidazole against biofilm infections through modulating the hypoxic microenvironment

coredata.dc:description: Bacteria within biofilm microenvironments experience distinct metabolic conditions that contribute to antibiotic resistance, posing a significant risk to human health. Altering these biofilm conditions can impact bacterial metabolism and potentially restore their sensitivity to antibiotics. In this context, a composite drug delivery system, referred to as OACC, was developed. This system integrates Ce6-modified carbon nanodots (CCe6) and gold nanocages for targeted delivery of ornidazole (ONZ) to biofilms. The mechanism study results indicated that OACC not only possesses photodynamic (PDT) and photothermal (PTT) antibacterial activity but also exacerbates the hypoxic microenvironment of biofilms, promoting bacteria to produce nitroreductase through anaerobic metabolic pathways, thereby activating the antibacterial activity of ONZ. Therefore, OACC exhibited extremely strong antibacterial (99.9 %) and anti-biofilm (96.1 %) activities. In addition, OACC rapidly cleared biofilms from infected wounds in diabetic mice, recruited M2-like macrophages to secrete anti-inflammatory factors, and induced high expression of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (CD31), which promoted vascular remodeling, collagen deposition, and wound healing in the infected wounds. The approach of combining PTT, and PDT, and intensifying the biofilm's hypoxic environment to enhance antibiotic efficacy offers an innovative strategy for repurposing antibiotics and addressing biofilm infections.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the OACC synthesis process, triple antibacterial and antibiofilm strategies (PTT, PDT, and activation of ONZ activity), and mechanisms that promote wound healing in diabetic infections.

Synthesis and characterization of OACC. (A) Synthesis of Ce6-modified carbon nanodots and surface modification of ornidazole-rich gold nanocages. TEM images of CDs (B), Scale bar: 5 nm. TEM (Scale bar: 5 nm) and HRTEM (Scale bar: 2 nm) images of CCe6 (C). TEM (Scale bar: 200 nm) and HRTEM (Scale bar: 50 nm) images of OAu (D). TEM (Scale bar: 200 nm), HRTEM (Scale bar: 50 nm), and local magnified (Scale bar: 10 nm) images of OACC (E). (F) FTIR spectra of Au NCs, CDs, CCe6, OAu and OACC. (G) UV−vis absorption spectra of Au NCs, Ce6, CDs, CCe6, ONZ, OAu and OACC. (H) XRD spectra of OACC. High-resolution XPS spectra of OACC (I) and its C 1 s (J), Au 4f (K), and N 1 s (L) orbitals. (M) 1O2 yields of CDs, Ce6, CCe6, and OACC determined by ESR spectroscopy after 5 min of laser irradiation. (N) Infrared thermal images of OACC (0.5 mg/mL) following irradiation at 0.5–2 W/cm2 for 0–10 min, respectively. (O) Temperature changes in pure water, OAu, CCe6, and OACC (0.5 mg/mL) under 660 nm laser irradiation at 1 W/cm2 for 0–10 min. (P) Temperature change curves after three irradiation cycles of OACC (0.5 mg/mL).

Investigation of in vitro photodynamic and photothermal antimicrobial activity of OACC. Concentration-dependent antimicrobial activity of ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC with (A) and without (B) 660 nm laser treatment for 5 min. (C) Effect of OACC on bacterial growth curves with and without 660 nm laser treatment for 5 min. The antimicrobial efficiencies of ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC with and without 660 nm laser treatment were examined by the CFU method and demonstrated by typical representative images (D) and colony counting results (E) (n = 3, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (F) PI and SYTO9 were used for fluorescence imaging of dead/living bacteria before and following laser treatment, Scale bar: 10 μm. (G) Statistical results of PI relative fluorescence intensity (n = 3, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (H) Effects of ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC on bacterial survival after irradiation with or without laser light were examined by flow cytometry, respectively.

OACC exhibited laser-enhanced anti-biofilm activity. (A) Schematic diagram of the transwell assay to assess the permeability of bacterial biofilms to ONZ and CCe6. (B) Relative release rates of ONZ from biofilms before and after laser irradiation. (C) Quantitative analysis of bacterial biomass in biofilms in various treatment groups with and without 660 nm laser treatment. (D) Crystal violet staining of MRSA biofilm with and without laser treatment under each treatment group, Scale bar: 1 mm. (E) Bacterial biofilm removal in each treatment group (n = 3, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (F) Effect of treatment groups on eDNA content in bacterial biofilms (n = 3, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). SEM (G) and 3D CLSM (H) images of bacterial biofilms before and after ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC laser treatments, Scale bar: 5 μm.

Anti-biofilm mechanism of OACC. CLSM images of [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (A) and SOSG (B) stained MRSA biofilms with and without laser treatments by ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC, Scale bar: 10 μm. Relative levels of 1O2 (C) and O2 (D) in biofilms with and without laser treatment in various treatment groups (n = 3, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). The absorbance of nitrofurantoin (E) and nitrofurazone (F) at 420 nm and 400 nm, respectively after incubation with cell extracts of MRSA biofilm in various treatment groups (n = 3, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). SEM (G) and TEM (H) images of MRSA in each treatment group after laser treatment and without laser treatment. (I) Schematic representation of the MRSA biofilm treatment process, illustrating CCe6 and ONZ release, hypoxia induction, and ROS generation by PDT, ONZ activation, photothermal therapy, and biofilm clearance.

RNA sequencing to analyze the antibacterial and anti-biofilm molecular mechanisms of OACC. (A) DEGs identified in MRSA transcriptome analysis in the control and OACC laser treatment groups for PCA analysis. Scatterplot (B) and volcano plot (C) analysis of DEGs in the control and OACC laser treatment groups (gray: no differentially expressed genes; red: up-regulated genes; blue: down-regulated genes). (D) GO enrichment analysis of DEGs. (E) KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of DEGs. (F) Heat map analysis of DEGs involved in biofilm formation, oxidoreductase activity, energy metabolism, and DNA damage repair proteins.

OACC-based synergistic PDT and PTT of MRSA biofilm-infected wounds in diabetic mice. (A) Schematic representation of MRSA biofilm infection, wound model construction, and treatment process. Thermograms (B) and temperature profiles (C) of mice after 0–5 min of 660 nm laser irradiation following in situ injection of nanopreparations into wound tissue. (D) Relative biomass of infected wounds on day 2 following treatment with nanopreparations (n = 5, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). Photographs (E), wound areas (F), and healing schematics (G) of infected wounds after treatment, Scale bar: 0.5 cm, (n = 5, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

The mechanism underlying OACC's enhanced wound healing of infected diabetic wounds. (A) Immunofluorescence images of F4/80 (red), CD206 (green), CD80 (purple), and DAPI (blue) in infected wounds on day 10 after PDT and PTT treatment, Scale bar: 100 μm. CD80 (B) and CD206 (C) area as a percentage of F4/80 area calculated from immunofluorescence images (n = 5, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (D) H&E staining, Masson staining, immunohistochemical staining for TNF-α, TGF-β, and CD31 of infected wound tissues on day 10 after treatment in each treatment group. Percentage of area positive for TNF-α (E), TGF-β (F), collagen density (G), and CD31 (H) expression calculated from immunohistochemical or immunofluorescence images (n = 5, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (I) The expression levels of HIF-1α and VEGF in infected tissues determined following treatment by WB. Relative quantitative analysis of the expression levels of HIF-1α (J) and VEGF (K) performed based on WB results (n = 3, mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001). (L) Schematic representation of the mechanisms by which OACC promotes healing of diabetic infected wounds, including removal of wound bacterial biofilm, recruitment of M2 macrophages, secretion of anti-inflammatory factors, acceleration of collagen regeneration, and promotion of angiogenesis.

head.ce:title: Gold nanocage-carbon nanodot composites activate ornidazole against biofilm infections through modulating the hypoxic microenvironment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacteria within biofilm microenvironments experience distinct metabolic conditions that contribute to antibiotic resistance, posing a significant risk to human health. Altering these biofilm conditions can impact bacterial metabolism and potentially restore their sensitivity to antibiotics. In this context, a composite drug delivery system, referred to as OACC, was developed. This system integrates Ce6-modified carbon nanodots (CCe6) and gold nanocages for targeted delivery of ornidazole (ONZ) to biofilms. The mechanism study results indicated that OACC not only possesses photodynamic (PDT) and photothermal (PTT) antibacterial activity but also exacerbates the hypoxic microenvironment of biofilms, promoting bacteria to produce nitroreductase through anaerobic metabolic pathways, thereby activating the antibacterial activity of ONZ. Therefore, OACC exhibited extremely strong antibacterial (99.9 %) and anti-biofilm (96.1 %) activities. In addition, OACC rapidly cleared biofilms from infected wounds in diabetic mice, recruited M2-like macrophages to secrete anti-inflammatory factors, and induced high expression of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (CD31), which promoted vascular remodeling, collagen deposition, and wound healing in the infected wounds. The approach of combining PTT, and PDT, and intensifying the biofilm's hypoxic environment to enhance antibiotic efficacy offers an innovative strategy for repurposing antibiotics and addressing biofilm infections.
Gold nanocages deliver ornidazole and Ce6-modified carbon dots to biofilms
Photodynamic and photothermal synergistically modulate biofilm hypoxic microenvironment

Anaerobic metabolism generates nitroreductase to activate the antibacterial activity of ornidazole
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All other data are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial biofilm formation is a major factor that complicates the treatment of chronic infections, presenting a significant threat to both human health and public healthcare systems [1–4]. During biofilm development, bacteria engage in quorum sensing and secrete extracellular polymers (EPS) such as polysaccharides, proteins, and extracellular DNA (eDNA). This dense, protective matrix enables the bacteria to develop resistance to antibiotics [5–7]. In chronic wounds infected with biofilms, these biofilms perpetuate long-term inflammation by persistently interacting with host immune cells and triggering the release of inflammatory mediators, which in turn disrupts the normal healing process [8,9]. Clinically, biofilm infections are primarily managed through antibiotics or surgical removal. However, the emergence of bacterial resistance and the development of secondary infections often result in treatment failure [10–12]. Thus, there is a critical need for a therapeutic approach that effectively disrupts biofilms and minimizes the risk of bacterial resistance.

The bacterial biofilm microenvironment is characterized by an acidic pH, low oxygen levels (hypoxia), overproduction of specific enzymes, and active quorum sensing [13,14]. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), a facultative anaerobe, exhibits different metabolic behaviors depending on its location within the biofilm. MRSA on the outer layers of the biofilm utilizes aerobic metabolism due to the presence of oxygen [15,16]. In contrast, MRSA within the biofilm's interior relies on anaerobic metabolism because of the limited oxygen, leading to a generally inactive metabolic state and increased resistance. This intrinsic multidrug resistance and reduced metabolic activity render antibiotics less effective [17–20]. Ornidazole (ONZ), a third-generation nitroimidazole antibiotic, is typically activated by nitroreductase, converting it into an amine derivative that damages bacterial DNA and induces cell death [21,22]. Although ONZ is highly effective against anaerobic bacteria, it is usually ineffective against facultative anaerobes in normal oxygen conditions [23,24]. However, in hypoxic environments, MRSA can upregulate nitroreductase through anaerobic metabolism, potentially enhancing the antibacterial activity of ONZ.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) have emerged as effective non-resistant antimicrobial strategies in the post-antibiotic era [25,26]. Under oxygen-rich conditions, PDT effectively eradicates bacteria by activating photosensitizers to generate singlet oxygen (1O2) [27,28]. However, the effectiveness of PDT is hindered by the photodegradation of photosensitizers under laser irradiation and the hypoxic environment of bacterial biofilms, which reduces 1O2 production [29,30]. Mou et al. developed a biofilm microenvironment-responsive nanoplatform (MAPC) that enhances antimicrobial sonodynamic therapy (aSDT) by promoting biofilm degradation, improving sonosensitizer penetration, and relieving hypoxia, achieving significant antibacterial effects both in vitro and in vivo against MRSA biofilm infections [31]. Dong et al. developed an enzyme-like photocatalytic nanomaterial, Rh/Ag2MoO4, which combines photothermal and photocatalytic antibacterial therapy to eliminate pathogens and catalyzes hydrogen peroxide to alleviate hypoxia, demonstrating excellent therapeutic potential for diabetic-infected wounds in vivo [32]. Although this strategy significantly enhances the antibacterial efficacy of photodynamic or sonodynamic therapy by alleviating the hypoxic microenvironment of biofilms, its antibacterial effects are difficult to sustain throughout the wound healing process, failing to meet the requirements for long-term antibacterial activity. Combining PDT, PTT, and antibiotic treatment may represent a more effective strategy for long-term antibacterial and anti-biofilm infection therapy.

In this study, a synergistic strategy combining PDT, PTT, and antibiotic activation was proposed to integrate the instantaneous bactericidal activity of photodynamic therapy with the long-term antibacterial activity of antibiotics. The photosensitizer Ce6 was immobilized onto carbon nanodots (CDs) via amide condensation, forming the composite CCe6 with PDT activity for generating singlet oxygen (1O2). Hollow gold nanocages (Au NCs) were synthesized by etching silver nanocubes through a displacement reaction and loaded with ornidazole (ONZ) to create OAu. CCe6 was electrostatically adsorbed onto the surface of OAu, resulting in the formation of OACC, where the combination of CDs and Au NCs provided enhanced PTT activity. OACC's PDT activity generated singlet oxygen (1O2) to inactivate bacteria, while its PTT-induced heat further amplified the antibacterial and antibiofilm effects. Additionally, PDT intensified biofilm hypoxia, inducing MRSA to produce nitroreductase via anaerobic metabolism, which activated ONZ to damage bacterial DNA. OACC laser therapy may accelerate the healing of infected wounds in diabetic mice by rapid removal of biofilms, recruitment of M2-like macrophages that secrete anti-inflammatory factors, angiogenesis promotion, collagen deposition, and tissue remodeling (as illustrated in Scheme 1 ). This work confirmed the proposed hypothesis and highlighted the potential of combining PDT, PTT, and antibiotic activation to modulate the biofilm microenvironment. This approach offers new insights into antibiotic repurposing and the treatment of biofilm-based infections.

All chemicals do not require further purification if not otherwise specified. Citric acid (≥99.5 %), 4-(4,6-dimethoxy-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)−4-methylmorpholinium chloride (DMT-MM, ≥97 %), polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW = 600, ≥99.0 %), polyvinylpyrrolidone K30 (PVP-k30, ≥99.0 %), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, ≥99.0 %), AgNO3 (≥99.0 %), HAuCl4
· 3H2O (≥99.0 %), chlorin e6 (Ce6, (≥94.0 %), [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (≥95.0 %) were obtained from Macklin; Ornidazole (ONZ, ≥98.0 %), nitrofurantoin (≥98.0 %) and nitrofurazone (≥98.0 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich; LIVE/DEAD BacLight™ bacterial viability kits, singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) were obtained from ThermoFisher Scientific; Anti-TGF-beta1 antibody (TGF-β1, ab315254), Anti-TNF-αlpha antibody (TNF-α, ab307164), Anti-CD206 antibody (ab64693), Anti-CD80 antibody (ab254579), Anti-CD31 antibody (ab281583), Anti-VEGF antibody (ab315238), Anti-HIF-1 alpha antibody (ab1) and Anti-F4/80 antibody (ab300421) were obtained from Abcam. MRSA (ATCC 43,300) was provided by the School of Pharmacy, Bengbu Medical University.

Citric acid (1 g) and PEI (0.65 g) were dissolved in formamide (20 mL). The mixed solution was then transferred to a reactor for 4 h (160 °C). When the reaction was completed and cooled to 25 °C, impurities were removed by centrifugation (6000 g, 5 min) and then a 0.22 μm filter membrane was used to remove smaller particles from the solution. The reaction solution was dialyzed (Molecular Weight Cut Off: 2000 Da) for 3 days to adequately remove solvents and small molecule impurities, and then freeze-dried to obtain amino-rich CDs. Further, Ce6-modified CDs were further prepared. Briefly, Ce6 (25 mg) and DMT-MM (52 mg) were dissolved in methanol (65 mL) and then a DMSO solution (5 mL) of CDs (50 mg) was added. After stirring the reaction for 24 h, the reaction solution was dialyzed for 3 days to remove Ce6 and DMT-MM, and CCe6 was collected after freeze-drying.

The synthesis of Au NCs was based on a slight modification of our previously reported method [33]. Briefly, PVP-K30 (650 mg), AgNO3 (340 mg) and NaCl (10 mg) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (7 mL) and then glycerol (2 mL) was added and reacted at 150 °C for 2 h to obtain Ag nanocubes. The Ag nanocube solution (1 mL) was dispersed in aqueous CTAB (0.1 mg/mL, 100 mL) and boiled for 10 min, then HAuCl4
· 3H2O (1.97 mg/mL, 5 mL) was added slowly and the heating reaction was continued for 20 min. The reaction products were washed repeatedly with saturated sodium chloride solution to adequately remove the AgCl precipitate from the displacement reaction, and then centrifuged (10,000 g, 10 min) to collect the Au NCs. Finally, an aqueous solution of Au NCs (1 mg/mL, 10 mL) and a DMSO solution of ONZ (10 mg/mL, 10 mL) were mixed and stirred for the reaction. After 24 h, the products were washed three times with pure water, centrifuged (10,000 g, 10 min) and freeze-dried to obtain ONZ-loaded gold nanocages (OAu). Further, to obtain CCe6-modified OAu, the CCe6 solution (10 mg/mL, 2 mL) was slowly added to the OAu solution (2 mg/mL, 10 mL), and under constant sonication and stirring, the CCe6 was modified on the surface and in the porous structure of the OAu by electrostatic action. Unloaded CCe6 was removed by repeated centrifugation (10,000 g, 10 min) and washing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of ONZ and Ce6 loading rates
ce:section.ce:para:
To calculate the loading of ONZ and CCe6 in OACC, their standard curves were firstly plotted at ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) absorbance 319 nm and 405 nm, respectively. Then, dialysate and post-centrifugation supernatant were collected during the synthesis of OACC and OAu, respectively. After the volume was fixed to 1000 mL, the absorbance at 405 nm and 319 nm was measured, and the contents of CCe6 and ONZ were calculated from the standard curves. Finally, the effective loading of CCe6 and ONZ was calculated according to the following equations:

ce:section.ce:section-title: OACC in vitro antibacterial activity evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of nanoformulations against MRSA was determined using the broth microdilution method provided by the Clinical Laboratory Standards Institute [34,35]. Briefly, 100 μL of bacterial dilutions were added to 96-well plates containing 100 μL of different concentrations of ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC (OACC without ONZ) and OACC solutions. The treated bacteria were then treated under laser irradiation (660 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions for 5 min and continued to be incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Bacterial colony forming units (CFU) were recorded in the culture solution of each treatment group by smear plate method and MIC was defined as the concentration of nanoformulation in the absence of any bacterial growth. Furthermore, Bacterial growth was observed every hour by reading the optical density at 600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging and flow cytometric analysis of live/dead bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The LIVE/DEAD BacLight™ bacterial viability kit was used to detect the extent of bacterial damage caused by nanoformulation before and after laser treatment. ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC (3.2 mg/mL, 100 μL) were added to 900 μL of bacterial dilution, followed by treatment under laser irradiation (660 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions for 5 min and continued incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, bacteria were washed, collected by centrifugation and stained by a mixture of SYTO9 (5 μM) and PI (30 μM) fluorescent dyes for 15 min in the dark. Finally, fluorescence imaging and quantitative analysis of live/dead bacteria were recorded by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and flow cytometry, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of ONZ permeability in MRSA biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Permeability of released ONZ to MRSA biofilms was assessed before and after OACC laser treatment. Briefly, MRSA was inoculated in Transwell inserts and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h to form biofilms. Then, the PBS dispersion of OACC was transferred to the insert and co-incubated with the biofilm for 4 h. This was followed by treatment under laser irradiation (660 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions for 5 min, and incubation was continued for 20 h at 37 °C. The amount of ONZ that penetrated into the receiver plate was calculated at intervals based on the UV-visible absorption spectra and the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro analysis of anti-biofilm activity
ce:section.ce:para: To test the ability of the nanoformulations to remove biofilms, 320 μg/mL of ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC solutions were added to the pre-formed biofilms. Meanwhile, they were treated under laser irradiation (660 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions for 5 min and continued to be incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Biofilms were fixed by methanol for 20 min and then stained by 0.1 % crystal violet for 15 min, after which they were washed at least three times by PBS. Finally, the crystal violet in the biofilm was dissolved by ethanol and absorbance was recorded at 590 nm. Similarly, bacteria within MRSA biofilms after different treatments were stained using a mixture of SYTO9 and PI dyes. The biofilm was then imaged with CLSM to obtain its 3D structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of eDNA and extracellular polysaccharides (EPS) in biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: The MRSA biofilms were formed in a 24-well plate. After synergistic laser treatment with various nanoformulations, the biofilm was separated by ultrasonic and vortex treatment. Further, bacterial cells were completely removed by centrifugation (5000 g, 5 min) and utilizing a 0.22 μm filter membrane to collect the supernatant. Finally, the concentration of eDNA was measured using the NanoDrop 2000c spectrophotometer. In addition, the supernatant of biofilm (10 mL) from each treatment group was mixed with an equal volume of phenol solution (50 mg/mL). Take 1 mL of the mixed solution and then immediately add 5 mL of 95 % sulfuric acid and incubate for 30 min at room temperature. After the reaction was completed, the absorbance at 490 nm was measured and the content of EPS was calculated from the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of hypoxia and monoclinic oxygen (1O2) levels in biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: ONZ, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC (320 μg/mL) were added to biofilms pre-formed in 96-well plates and then treated under laser irradiation (660 nm, 1 W/cm2) or dark conditions for 5 min. The treated biofilms were immediately stained with [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (10 μg/mL) for 6 h. Subsequently, the biofilm was washed three times with PBS to remove excess dye. Finally, bacteria in the biofilm were extracted by ultrasound and fluorescence images of MRSA were obtained using CLSM. To detect the level of 1O2 in the biofilm, the biofilm was fluorescently stained by using 10 μM SOSG for 30 min. Consistent with the method described above, bacteria in the biofilm were extracted after PBS washing and fluorescence imaging was performed by CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitroreductase activity in MRSA
ce:section.ce:para: The nitroreductase activity of MRSA in biofilms was analyzed according to the methodology of the study reported by previous authors [19]. Briefly, various nanoformulations (320 μg/mL) were added to the MRSA biofilm, treated for 5 min under laser irradiation or dark conditions, and continued to be incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. MRSA was collected from the biofilm by ultrasound and centrifugation, washed three times with Tris–HCl buffer at 4 °C, and incubated with 50 μg/mL lysostaphin and 50 μg/mL deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I) for 30 min, respectively. Nitroreductase activity in MRSA extracts was determined using 50 μM nitrofurantoin and 50 μM nitrofurazone as substrates and 250 μM nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) as electron donor. The absorbance of nitrofurazone and nitrofurantoin at wavelengths of 400 nm and 420 nm, respectively, was determined to evaluate the nitroreductase activity of MRSA in biofilms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of biofilm-infected wounds in diabetic mice
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental animals were specific pathogen free (SPF) grade KM male mice (6–8 weeks old) provided by Jiangsu Lingfei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The animal experimental procedures were conducted in strict accordance with the recommendations in the Chinese Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals and the Animal Research: Reporting of in vivo Experiments (ARRIVE) guidelines. The protocols were approved by the Committee on the Ethics for Animal Experiments of Bengbu Medical University (Permit Number:2022–289), and all mice were euthanized after the experiment. Mice were injected intraperitoneally with 60 mg/kg of streptozotocin every 3 days for 2–3 weeks until the fasting blood glucose level of the mice was higher than 16.7 mM, indicating that the diabetic mouse model was successfully constructed. After anesthetizing the mice through sodium pentobarbital (50 mg/kg), a circular incision of approximately 1.0 cm in diameter was excised from their backs and injected with MRSA biofilm dispersion (107 CFU), which was incubated with the wounds for 24 h (wound coverage with 3 M Tegaderm semiocclusive transparent films) to create diabetic biofilm-infected wounds. Subsequently, 320 μg/mL of OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC (100 μL) were injected into the wounds (an equal volume of PBS was injected as a control group) and treated for 5 min under laser irradiation or dark conditions. Meanwhile, the temperature changes of the mice wounds before and after laser irradiation were recorded by a photothermal imaging system. In addition, the healing rate of diabetic infected wounds was recorded on days 2, 4, 6 and 10 post-treatment and the in vivo antibacterial rate of the nanoformulations was assessed by plate counting method.

After 10 days of different treatments, the biofilm-infected wound tissues of diabetic mice were collected and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, then embedded in paraffin and sectioned. Sections were stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) or Masson for the distribution of inflammatory cells and collagen deposition in the infected tissues of each treatment group, respectively. For immunofluorescence staining, tissue sections were incubated with 3 % methanol for 10 min and 2 % bovine serum albumin (BSA) for 30 min, respectively. The tissue sections were then incubated with primary antibody at 4 °C overnight before being incubated with fluorescently labeled secondary antibody, including Alexa Fluor488-IgG (Abcam, ab150157), Alexa Fluor594-IgG (Abcam, ab150160), and tetramethylrhodamine (TRITC)-IgG (Jackson ImmunoResearch, 115–025–062) for 1 h. Finally, cell nuclei were stained by 4′, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and imaged by pathological section scanner (Olympus VS200).

All experiments were performed at least three times and data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The statistical analysis was performed using OriginPro 8.0, followed by a Student's t-test and one-way analysis of variance. Probability (P) values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant, where *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001.

In contrast to the development of new antibiotics, the rational design of the current ones can reduce bacterial resistance to antibiotics, thereby improving their antimicrobial efficiency [35,36]. First, amino-rich CDs were synthesized by the thermal reaction of citric acid and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in formamide. The photosensitizer Ce6 was then coupled to the CDs via an amide condensation reaction to obtain Ce6-modified CDs (CCe6). Gold nanocages were fabricated using a previously established method and were then filled with ornidazole within their cage-like structure (OAu). The loading rates of Ce6 on CDs, ONZ on Au NCs, and CCe6 on OAu were calculated to be 6.5 %, 21.7 %, and 18.2 %, respectively, based on the regression equations (Figures S1A and S1B). Finally, CCe6 was electrostatically adsorbed onto the OAu surface to create the OACC composite nanosystem (Fig. 1 A). This modification was evidenced by a change in the color of the sample solution (Figure S2). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that CDs (Fig. 1B) and CCe6 (Fig. 1C) appeared as uniformly dispersed spherical particles with diameters of approximately 3.5 nm and 4.0 nm, respectively (Figure S3A). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images revealed that the lattice spacing of CCe6 is 0.21 nm, matching the graphene (100) facet. As illustrated in Figs. 1D and 1E, OAu exhibited a hollow cubic structure, and the successful modification of CCe6 on the OACC surface was evident by a slight increase in diameter. Subsequently, the surface charge of the samples was evaluated. The results showed a change in zeta potential during each step of the synthesis, which is indicative of successful surface modification (Figure S3B). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra in Fig. 1F show that the absorption peaks of OACC at 1650 cm-1 and 3400 cm-1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = O and −NH2 on the surface of CCe6, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1490 cm-1 and 1710 cm-1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of N = O (-NO2) in ONZ. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1G) exhibit absorption peaks at 319 nm and 520 nm, corresponding to ONZ and CDs, respectively. The peaks at 405 nm and 660 nm are attributed to Ce6 and Au NCs. The crystal structure of OACC was further analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 1H. The broad peak centered at 2θ = 23.5° indicates a graphite structure, while the crystal diffraction peak matches that of cubic gold (JCPDS No 04–0784). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) image (Fig. 1I) of OACC shows the presence of major elements such as C, N, O, and Au. As shown in Fig. 1J, the high-resolution XPS image of C 1 s can be fitted to four binding energies at 284.6, 285.7, 287.9, and 289.6 eV attributed to CC/C = C, CN, C = O, and O = CO, respectively [37]. Fig. 1K shows the binding energy peaks of Au 4f at 83.8 and 87.6 eV, which can be attributed to Au0 4f7/2 and Au0 4f5/2, respectively [38]. Fig. 1L shows that the XPS image of N 1 s can be resolved into pyridine N (399.7 eV) and amino N (402.1 eV) [39]. These characterization results are in agreement with the theoretical values, indicating the successful synthesis of OACC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PDT and PTT characteristics of OACC
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the ROS production and photothermal efficiency of each component under laser irradiation, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy, and photothermal imaging systems were employed to monitor singlet oxygen (1O2) generation and temperature changes, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1M, characteristic spectra of 1O2 were detected for CDs, Ce6, CCe6, and OACC after laser irradiation (660 nm, 1W/cm², 5 min). The 1O2 yield of CCe6 and OACC is similar but significantly higher than that of Ce6 or CDs alone, which was attributed to the synergistic effect between Ce6 and CDs. This may be more conducive to utilizing the oxygen within bacterial biofilms to produce singlet oxygen. Fig. 1N demonstrate that the temperature of the OACC solution increased with both higher laser power and prolonged irradiation time. The most significant temperature rise was observed at a laser power of 1 W/cm² and an irradiation time of 5 min, beyond which further increases in laser power and time had minimal effect on the solution temperature. Importantly, OAu and CCe6 exhibited synergistically enhanced photothermal effects under laser irradiation, with OACC achieving the highest temperature (Fig. 1O). In addition, 320 μg/mL of OACC increased its temperature to nearly 50 °C after 5 min of laser irradiation (Figure S4). After three "heating/cooling" irradiation cycles, the PTT performance of OACC remained stable, indicating excellent PTT stability (Fig. 1P). In summary, OACC exhibits. The synergistic enhancement in PDT and PTT effects is likely due to the simultaneous generation of ROS by Ce6 and CDs under 660 nm laser excitation, coupled with the photothermal effects of Au NCs and CDs under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-MRSA activity of OACC
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), colony forming units (CFU), live/dead bacterial staining, and flow cytometry were used to assess the in vitro antimicrobial activity of OACC, before and after laser irradiation. The MIC results are presented in Figs. 2 A and 2B, demonstrating that the MIC value of OACC decreased to 320 μg/mL following laser irradiation, compared to that observed with the antibiotic ONZ (>1280 μg/mL). This value is significantly lower than the MIC values of CCe, OAu, and ACC. Consequently, the study focused on the antimicrobial activity of the 320 μg/mL nanoformulation against MRSA. Laser irradiation alone had no significant impact on the growth curve of MRSA, which retained its typical "S" shape. However, OACC, after laser irradiation, completely inhibited MRSA growth (Fig. 2C). The antibacterial activity of the nanoformulations was further assessed using the CFU method. The antibiotic ONZ exhibited negligible antimicrobial activity against MRSA, presumably because ONZ is effective against anaerobes expressing nitroreductase activity, but has no activity against facultative anaerobes and aerobes under normoxic conditions. In contrast, OAu (46.9 %), CCe6 (69.2 %), ACC (86.2 %), and OACC (99.9 %) demonstrated significantly higher antimicrobial activity following laser irradiation (Figs. 2D and 2E). The in vitro antimicrobial activity of OACC was also further evaluated using SYTO9/PI fluorescent staining. As shown in Fig. 2F, the majority of MRSA in the non-laser-treated group displayed green fluorescence, indicating intact bacterial membranes. In contrast, following laser treatment, green fluorescence was scarcely visible, with OACC (laser) showing the strongest PI red fluorescence (Fig. 2G). This suggests that OACC (Laser) caused the most significant damage to the bacterial cell membrane. These findings are corroborated by the results of the flow cytometric assay (Fig. 2H), which demonstrated that OACC (laser) had stronger antimicrobial activity compared to ONZ (laser), CCe6 (laser), OAu (laser), and ACC (laser). The highest antimicrobial activity exhibited by OACC under laser irradiation resulted from the significant disruption of the cell membrane of MRSA. This enhanced antimicrobial effect is likely due to the synergistic interactions of PDT, PTT, and antibiotics.

Biofilms are a major barrier that prevents antibiotics from penetrating, contributing to the development of bacterial drug resistance. Consequently, disrupting bacterial biofilms is an effective strategy for combating drug-resistant bacterial infections [40–42]. First, the permeability of ONZ released from OACC to biofilm before and after laser irradiation was investigated by the Transwell method. As shown in Figs. 3 A and 3B, ONZ in OACC was released and traversed the MRSA biofilm more quickly after laser irradiation, with 74.97 % of ONZ eventually crossing the biofilm. In contrast, about 68.37 % of ONZ crossed the biofilm in the group without laser irradiation. This indicates that laser irradiation facilitates ONZ release and penetration through the biofilm. The biofilm scavenging ability of the nanoformulations was further assessed using crystal violet staining, SEM, and 3D fluorescence imaging. The crystal violet staining results in Figs. 3C and 3E showed limited biofilm removal by the treatment groups without laser irradiation. In contrast, OAu, CCe6, ACC, and OACC demonstrated 20.6 %, 55.9 %, 72.7 %, and 96.1 % removal of MRSA biofilm following laser treatment, respectively, as evidenced by a significant decrease in the absorbance of crystal violet at 590 nm (Fig. 3D). SEM results confirmed that the biofilm structure in the blank group was compact. However, the extracellular polymers in the biofilm, primarily comprising extracellular polysaccharides (Figures S5A and S5B) and eDNA (Fig. 3F), and the bacterial biomass decreased significantly after OACC (laser) treatment (Fig. 3G). The 3D confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of the biofilm (Fig. 3H) demonstrated that the MRSA biofilm in the control and non-laser-treated groups was densely populated with live cells (green fluorescence). However, laser treatment disrupted the biofilm structure to varying extents across different treatment groups. The most significant disruption was observed in the OACC (laser) group, where nearly all bacteria in the biofilm were dead (red fluorescence). It is worth noting that under this high-glycemic environment, OACC exhibits significant anti-biofilm activity after irradiation, suggesting that it has the potential to treat biofilm infections in the hyperglycemic microenvironment of diabetic wounds. It was hypothesized that the antimicrobial and anti-biofilm activities of CCe6, OAu, and ACC can be respectively attributed to the production of singlet oxygen, photothermal activity, and the synergistic interactions between PDT and PTT. Furthermore, OACC (laser) demonstrated the strongest antibacterial and anti-biofilm effects, likely due to PDT depleting oxygen in the biofilm microenvironment and promoting high expression of nitroreductase by MRSA in the biofilm. This process may result in more effective bacterial killing through the activation of ONZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial and anti-biofilm mechanisms of OACC
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the above hypothesis, the antibacterial and anti-biofilm mechanisms of the nanoformulations before and after laser irradiation were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, laser irradiation of CCe6, ACC, and OACC led to the production of a significant amount of 1O2 by MRSA in the biofilm. This production was primarily due to the PDT activity of CCe6, which consumes oxygen to generate 1O2 (Fig. 4C). ACC generated more 1O2 than CCe6, likely due to the synergistic photothermal sensitization effects of Au NCs and CDs. Moreover, the oxygen levels within the MRSA biofilm decreased significantly after laser irradiation, as evidenced by the increased fluorescence intensity of [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 (Figs. 4B and 4D). Hypoxic microenvironments in biofilms typically induce bacteria to enter an anaerobic metabolic state, which contributes to drug resistance and reduced antibiotic efficacy. However, ONZ in OACC is activated by nitroreductase under hypoxic conditions, producing amine derivatives that damage bacterial DNA. Therefore, nitroreductase levels in MRSA biofilms were further investigated using nitrofurantoin and nitrofurazolidone. As shown in Figs. 4E and 4F, the nitroreductase activity of MRSA in biofilms increased significantly following laser treatment in combination with CCe6, ACC, and OACC. The increase was negatively correlated with the oxygen levels in the biofilm and positively correlated with the levels of 1O2. Further examination of MRSA morphology in the biofilm before and after laser treatment was conducted using SEM and TEM analysis. As illustrated in Figs. 4G and 4H, the OACC laser treatment caused extensive disruption of the cell membrane and cell wall of MRSA in the biofilm compared to OAu, CCe6, and ACC treatments. This disruption resulted in significant bacterial damage and the exocytosis of cellular constituents. In contrast to previously reported strategies that enhance the antibacterial and anti-biofilm effects of photosensitizers/sonosensitizers by improving the hypoxic microenvironment of biofilms [43,44]. This work exacerbates the hypoxic microenvironment within biofilms through the photodynamic activity of OACC, which induces MRSA to produce nitroreductase through anaerobic metabolic pathways and activate ONZ, resulting in a significant enhancement of antibacterial and antibiofilm activities (Fig. 4I). This approach provides new ideas for the reuse of antibiotics and the treatment of biofilm infections.

Transcriptome sequencing was carried out to analyze the antibacterial and anti-biofilm molecular mechanisms in OACC after laser treatment. The principal component analysis (PCA) results of MRSA differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in the biofilm of the control and OACC groups are presented in Fig. 5 A. The principal components showed consistency within each group but differed between the groups, validating the transcriptome analysis results. Compared to the control group, 1638 genes were upregulated and 177 genes were downregulated in the OACC laser treatment group (Figs. 5B and 5C). Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analyses were performed on DEGs to identify the 15 most significantly affected pathways. Figs. 5D and 5E illustrate that OACC (laser) treatment significantly impacted various pathways, including cell wall biogenesis, imidazole-containing compound metabolic processes, oxidoreductase activity, quorum sensing, biofilm formation, DNA replication, and Staphylococcus aureus infection. The heat map of the DEGs (Fig. 5F) indicates that these pathway enrichments may be related to the significant upregulation of biofilm degradation genes (sak and HtrA) and nitrate reductase genes (narG and narH), as well as the downregulation of genes related to energy metabolism (lacB and lacD) by OACC (laser). The transition from a biofilm to a planktonic state makes MRSA more susceptible to antibiotics, heat, and reactive oxygen species (ROS), which is crucial for effectively eliminating the bacteria in the biofilm. As anticipated, OACC (laser) promotes complete bacterial inactivation by accelerating biofilm breakdown, disrupting bacterial energy metabolism, and causing redox imbalance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo resistance to biofilm infection and induction of wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent anti-biofilm activity and clear anti-biofilm mechanism of OACC (Laser), further in vivo evaluations were conducted to assess the anti-biofilm and anti-infection activities of OACC before and after laser irradiation. This was done by constructing a wound model of MRSA biofilm-infected diabetic mice (Fig. 6 A). Figs. 6B and 6C display the thermographic photographs and photothermal change curves for different treatment modes, respectively. Under laser irradiation, the temperatures of the ACC and OACC treatment groups increased from 32.4 °C to 49.2 °C and 32.5 °C to 49.7 °C, respectively, within 5 min. These temperature rises were significantly higher than those observed for CCe6 (32.3 °C to 43.1 °C) and OAu (32.9 °C to 46.2 °C), presumably due to the synergistic effect of CDs and Au NCs. After establishing the biofilm infection model and initiating nanoformulation intervention (day 0), the wounds of mice in all groups exhibited similar morphology and size. However, quantitative analysis revealed a dramatic decrease in bacterial survival at the wound site, from 98.9 % ± 3.00 % in the laser-treated group to 4.7 % ± 2.13 % in the OACC (laser)-treated group (Fig. 6D). By day 4 post-treatment, significant differences in the infected wound morphology and size were observed among the groups. The wounds in the OACC (laser) treatment group crusted rapidly, and the wound area was significantly reduced (Figs. 6E and 6G). By day 10, the infected wound area had decreased by 48.2 % ± 3.66 % in the infected group and 51.2 % ± 5.13 % in the laser-treated group. The healing rates in these groups were significantly lower compared to the ACC (81.4 % ± 3.88 %) and OACC (93.2 % ± 3.76 %) treatment groups (Fig. 6F). Although the PDT and PTT efficiencies of ACC were similar to those of OACC, the in vivo antimicrobial and wound healing-promoting activities of ACC were significantly lower. This difference may be due to the antimicrobial activity exhibited by ONZ, which is activated in the hypoxic microenvironment of the biofilm, aligning with the results of the in vitro antimicrobial mechanism studies. These results thus confirm the powerful anti-biofilm and wound healing-promoting effects of the OACC (Laser) regimen in vivo. This effectiveness is likely due to the synergistic action of OACC combining photothermal, photodynamic, and antibiotic activation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of OACC phototherapy to promote diabetic wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: For biofilm-infected diabetic wounds, prolonged bacterial stimulation of the infected tissue leads to a dramatic increase in the concentration of pro-inflammatory cytokines, which severely interferes with the normal repair process. Macrophage polarization has a key regulatory function at different stages of wound repair [45,46]. A series of analyses were conducted to further evaluate the effect of PDT- and PTT-activated chemotherapy on macrophage polarization in diabetic-infected wounds. As shown in Fig. 7 A, OACC laser treatment significantly decreased the level of M1-like macrophages (Fig. 7B) and increased the level of M2-like macrophages (Fig. 7C) in diabetic-infected wounds compared to other treatment groups. M1-like macrophages are associated with the production of inflammatory factors, whereas M2-like macrophages contribute to wound repair by secreting anti-inflammatory factors. To better comprehend the process, the levels of inflammatory and anti-inflammatory factors in the infected wounds were measured (Fig. 7D). The model group exhibited significantly higher levels of tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) and lower levels of transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β) compared to the other treatment groups. It was evident that diabetic wounds treated with OACC laser therapy had the lowest levels of TNF-α (Fig. 7E) and the highest levels of TGF-β (Fig. 7F), indicating a stronger polarization toward the M2 phenotype. H&E staining results corroborated these findings, showing an absence of inflammatory cells and the presence of intact epidermal structures in the OACC laser-treated group, unlike the model group. These results collectively suggest that OACC laser treatment effectively promotes macrophage polarization towards the M2 phenotype, enhancing wound healing by reducing inflammation and supporting tissue repair. Masson staining of wound tissue showed abnormal skin structure and loss of collagen fibers in the model group. On the 10th day after ACC or OACC laser treatment, the wounds showed massive collagen deposition, and the collagen density was increased by 5.3-fold and 6.5-fold, respectively, indicating that the damaged tissues had recovered (Fig. 7G).

The level of hypoxia in infected wounds before and following OACC laser treatment was further evaluated using the Western blot method to measure hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α). As shown in Figs. 7H and 7I, the overexpression of HIF-1α in the OACC laser treatment group indicates that PDT enhances hypoxia in biofilm-infected wounds. Also, since HIF-1α promotes angiogenesis at the wound site, the expression of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (CD31) was examined. Figs. 7J and 7K show that in comparison to the infected group, the levels of CD31 and VEGF were significantly higher in diabetic-infected wounds following OACC laser treatment, which correlated positively with HIF-1α levels. Biofilm-infected diabetic wounds typically accumulate large numbers of M1-like macrophages and inflammatory factors due to prolonged bacterial stimulation, hindering wound healing. OACC (Laser) aggravates the hypoxic microenvironment within the biofilm through the synergistic effects of PDT and PTT, activates antibiotics, and completely eradicates MRSA from infected wounds. Importantly, treated wounds showed increased accumulation of M2-like macrophages, which secrete anti-inflammatory factors that promote inflammation resolution. Collagen deposition and HIF-1α-mediated angiogenesis were observed, ultimately enhancing the healing of infected wounds.

In addition to therapeutic effectiveness, biosafety is a crucial factor in determining the potential application of nanoformulations. To assess this, the cytotoxicity of the nanoformulations was first evaluated using the CCK8 kit. As shown in Figure S6A, the survival rate of mouse embryonic fibroblasts (NIH-3T3) after OACC laser treatment was as high as 95 %. The effect of the nanoformulation on red blood cell rupture and dissolution was subsequently investigated through hemolysis experiments. Figure S6B shows that the hemolysis rate of OACC (laser)-treated red blood cells was <5 %. After the wound-healing experiments, blood samples and major organs were collected from the mice for routine blood tests and histological evaluation. Routine blood analysis indicated that the blood parameters of the mice in each treatment group were similar to those of the control group (Figure S7). As shown in Figure S8, H&E staining results revealed no significant damage or organic changes in the major organs of the mice across all treatment groups. These findings confirm the excellent biosafety of OACC (Laser) in treating biofilm-infected chronic wounds and demonstrate its immense potential for clinical translation.

In conclusion, an anti-MRSA biofilm infection nanosystem (ONZ@Au@CDs-Ce6, OACC) with synergistic PDT, PTT, and chemotherapeutic effects was successfully synthesized. The photosensitizer Ce6 was immobilized on the surface of carbon dots (CDs) via an amide condensation reaction and subsequently modified on antibiotic-loaded gold nanocages through electrostatic interactions. The synergistic interaction of CDs with gold nanocages and Ce6 significantly enhanced the PTT and PDT activities of OACC, leading to increased production of singlet oxygen (1O2) and greater oxygen depletion in biofilms. In the extremely hypoxic biofilm microenvironment, MRSA produces nitroreductase via anaerobic metabolism, thereby activating the antimicrobial activity of ornidazole. In vitro antimicrobial assays demonstrated that OACC (laser) treatment exhibited significant antimicrobial (99.9 %) and antibiofilm (96.1 %) activity. Mechanistic studies revealed that OACC generates 1O2, disrupts bacterial cell walls and membranes, and prevents the formation of extracellular polymers through the combined actions of PDT, PTT, and ornidazole activation, which is consistent with transcriptome analysis results. Furthermore, OACC laser therapy accelerates the reduction of inflammation in chronically infected wounds by rapidly removing biofilms from diabetic wounds, shortening the M1-like macrophage phase, recruiting M2-like macrophages, and promoting the secretion of anti-inflammatory factors. High expression levels of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (CD31) facilitated wound microvessel formation and collagen deposition, thereby promoting tissue remodeling and healing. This study highlights the potential of using synergistically enhanced PDT and PTT to exacerbate the hypoxic microenvironment of biofilms, promote anaerobic metabolism in bacteria, and activate antibiotics for efficient antibacterial and antibiofilm effects, offering promising solutions for treating resistant bacterial infections. Nevertheless, the synthesis of OACC involves multi-step chemical reactions and fine assembly of nanomaterials, and has not been fully evaluated for its long-term biosafety, which requires further improvement and research.
All of the authors involved in this study have agreed to its publication. The authors declare no competing interests.

Zekun Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yingying Li: Visualization, Conceptualization. Hanqing Zhao: Methodology. Fangyuan Chen: Methodology. Wenjun Shao: Formal analysis. Man Jiang: Data curation. Lu Zhang: Resources. Tao Ma: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Qingqing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources.
